summary: My name is Masao Hirata, born on May 31 in Meiji 36 in Kumamoto Prefecture. I grew up facing hardships after my mother died when I was in the fourth grade, working as a baby-sitter and farm laborer. At eighteen, I came to America in 1920 to reunite with my father, though our relationship was distant. I worked hard picking grapes in Fresno and later moved to Los Angeles, where I became a Christian and started a family.
cues: worked in Fresno and Los Angeles

summary: I was born and raised in Kumamoto Prefecture, specifically in Toshine-Mura Iwano, Shimomashi-Gun. It was a rural area where I lived with my grandparents after my mother died. My early memories of school and childhood hardships all took place there before I emigrated to America in Taishō 9.

I remember hearing about the Sino-Japanese War, but I don’t recall many details. What stood out more were the songs about the fall of the city of Chintao during that time. It was around the Taishō 7 or 8 period, just before I came to America.

World War I was a significant event during my youth. I recall songs about the fall of Chintao when Kowshu Bay was surrendered, which was a memorable time for us. This was around Taishō 7 or 8, just before I left for America in Taishō 9.

Chintao was a city whose fall during the Sino-Japanese War and World War I was commemorated in songs I heard as a child. This event was part of the news and stories circulating around Taishō 7 or 8, just before I emigrated to America.

The Taishō period was the time when I was growing up and eventually decided to come to America. I left Japan in Taishō 9, around 1920, after experiencing hardships in my childhood and adolescence. It was a period marked by events like World War I and the fall of Chintao, which I remember from my youth.

Kimi Motomura was my third-grade teacher, a woman who told us many stories during lunch time. I remember one story about iridescent insects that taught us a moral lesson. She was one of the few teachers I had who made a lasting impression during my elementary school days in Kumamoto Prefecture.

Mr. Funabara, who was a Shinto priest, was my teacher in the first and second grades. He taught me during my early school years in Japan before I faced hardships after my mother’s death. His role as a Shinto priest made his teaching unique in my early education.

I departed from Nagasaki on July 24, 1920, aboard the Kurea-Maru of the Toyo Steamship Company to come to America. Nagasaki was the port where my journey to the United States began, stopping at Kobe, Shimizu, and Yokohama before crossing the Pacific.

The ship I took to America stopped at Kobe after leaving Nagasaki. It was one of the ports of call on my journey across Japan before heading to San Francisco. Kobe was part of the route I traveled in July 1920.

Shimizu in Shizuoka Prefecture was another port where the ship stopped on my way to America. This was part of the journey that took me from Nagasaki through Kobe and Yokohama before crossing the Pacific Ocean. It was July 1920 when I passed through Shimizu.

Yokohama was the last Japanese port where the ship stopped before heading to San Francisco. We left Yokohama on July 27, 1920, beginning the trans-Pacific voyage. It was a significant point in my journey to America.

I arrived in San Francisco on August 13, 1920, after leaving Yokohama and stopping in Hawaii. My father was there to meet me for the first time, though I felt closer to my uncle. We stayed overnight in San Francisco before heading to Fresno, and I remember the cool weather there compared to the heat in Fresno.

Our ship stopped in Hawaii on the way from Yokohama to San Francisco in August 1920. It was part of the long journey across the Pacific Ocean that brought me to America. I remember the trip as a significant moment in my life.

The Kurea-Maru was the ship I took from Nagasaki to San Francisco in July and August 1920. It was operated by the Toyo Steamship Company and stopped at Kobe, Shimizu, Yokohama, and Hawaii before arriving in America. This voyage marked the beginning of my new life in the United States.

The Toyo Steamship Company operated the Kurea-Maru, the ship that carried me from Nagasaki to San Francisco in 1920. This company was responsible for my passage to America, which was a major turning point in my life. The journey included stops at several Japanese ports and Hawaii.

After arriving in San Francisco, my father and I traveled to Del Rey in Fresno, California, on August 14, 1920. I remember the extreme heat there, over 105 degrees, which was a stark contrast to the cool weather in San Francisco. It was in Fresno where I started working picking grapes shortly after arrival.

Fresno was where I first worked in America, picking grapes starting August 20, 1920. I lived in makeshift housing with other workers and worked very hard for good wages compared to Japan. Later, I attended a Buddhist Society boarding school in Fresno to learn and improve myself.

In October 1920, I entered the boarding school run by the Buddhist Society in Fresno to study, despite my limited English. The tuition was 250 dollars a year, which I worked hard to pay during the summer. I stayed there until June the following year, eager to learn and improve my situation.

My father went to Enkyo Valley to pick cucumbers after I arrived in America, but he became ill and could not return to Fresno as expected. I sent him money to help him come back from Arizona, which he appreciated deeply. This valley was part of my father's work and struggle in America.

Arizona was where my father was working and became ill after I arrived in America. I sent him money from Fresno so he could return home, and he was very grateful. This was a difficult time for us, as he was my only parent in America.

The Great Kantō earthquake happened in 1923, the same year anti-Japanese laws were passed in America. This event coincided with a difficult time for Japanese immigrants like me, as land inheritance was banned and immigration was restricted. It influenced my decision to leave farming and move to Los Angeles in 1924.

I moved to Los Angeles in 1924 after the depression and anti-Japanese laws made farming difficult. There, I worked on strawberry farms in Torrance and became involved with a church led by Rev. Katsuhiro Iseri. Los Angeles became my new home where I started a family and deepened my faith.

Rev. Katsuhiro Iseri was a pastor and friend of my father in Los Angeles. I met him at a farewell party in his church, which was my first encounter with Christianity. Later, I became a member of his church, believing that God led me there to become a Christian.

The younger brother of Rev. Katsuhiro Iseri graduated from Stanford University but could not find a job in America and was returning to Japan. I attended his farewell party, which was my first experience with the church. This connection indirectly influenced my spiritual journey.

In Los Angeles, I worked on a strawberry farm in Torrance owned by the elder brother of my friend Tohru Maeda. This was my first job after moving to the city in 1924. It was hard work but helped me support myself and my family.

Tohru Maeda was a friend from my hometown who came to America with me. His elder brother owned the strawberry farm in Torrance where I worked after moving to Los Angeles. We supported each other during those early years in America.

In 1931, Dr. Tashiro asked me to reclaim 40 acres of land in Cotura Valley, which he had purchased. Although I was reluctant to return to Fresno's heat, I accepted his request. This marked a new chapter in my farming career, focusing on grapefruit cultivation.

Dr. Tashiro was a man who bought land in Cotura Valley and asked me to reclaim it in 1931. He planned to grow grapefruits on part of the land and allowed me to use the rest. His encouragement helped me continue farming despite earlier hardships.

The 1932 Summer Olympics occurred during my early years in America, but I did not mention any direct involvement. It was a notable event in Los Angeles around the time I was establishing my life there.

World War II was a significant event that came after the period I described, but I did not share personal memories or experiences related to it in this interview. My story focuses mostly on the years before the war.
